coredata.dc:description: Although photocatalytic H2 production based on semiconductor materials has a wide potential application, it still facing challenges such as slow reaction kinetics or complex synthesis processes. To meet these challenges, the carbon dots loaded black g-C3N4 (CN-B-CDs) was synthesized by simple calcination method to achieve efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production. Photothermal imaging experiments confirmed the photothermal effect of CN-B and CDs as dual heat sources to increase the temperature of the composite system, thus improving the effective separation of photo-generated charges. In addition, multiple photocatalytic H2 production tests exhibited that CN-B-CDs photocatalysts not only have strong stability but also can accommodate a variety of complex water bodies, which displayed the potential for industrial application. This study combined the photothermal effect and the mechanism by which the CDs promote the charge transfer to design a new photocatalytic H2 production system and provided a new scheme for achieving efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production using carbon-based materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although photocatalytic H2 production based on semiconductor materials has a wide potential application, it still facing challenges such as slow reaction kinetics or complex synthesis processes. To meet these challenges, the carbon dots loaded black g-C3N4 (CN-B-CDs) was synthesized by simple calcination method to achieve efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production. Photothermal imaging experiments confirmed the photothermal effect of CN-B and CDs as dual heat sources to increase the temperature of the composite system, thus improving the effective separation of photo-generated charges. In addition, multiple photocatalytic H2 production tests exhibited that CN-B-CDs photocatalysts not only have strong stability but also can accommodate a variety of complex water bodies, which displayed the potential for industrial application. This study combined the photothermal effect and the mechanism by which the CDs promote the charge transfer to design a new photocatalytic H2 production system and provided a new scheme for achieving efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production using carbon-based materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) energy, as a renewable energy source, provides new possibilities for sustainable development in the future due to its advantages of high energy density, environmental protection and easy storage [1,2]. Among all H2 production technologies, semiconductor-based photocatalytic H2 production is considered one of the ideal strategies for future sustainable development, because it consumes only renewable solar energy and does not emit any pollutants [3,4]. In the past few decades, numerous photocatalysts have been developed for photocatalytic hydrogen production [5,6]. Among them, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN) photocatalyst has received much attention for its unique advantages (such as low toxicity, high stability and suitable band gap), and is praised as the second generation of key materials in the field of photocatalysis after TiO2 [7–10]. Unfortunately, due to the insufficient absorption of visible light and the poor charge separation efficiency of pristine g-C3N4, the photocatalytic H2 production rate is still difficult to meet the needs of current industrial applications [7,11–13]. In response to these deficiencies, the researchers have proposed various improvement strategies for modifying g-C3N4, including material modification and coupled external fields (e. g., magnetic, electric, stress and thermal fields, etc.) [14–16].

As a common external field-assisted strategy in chemical reactions, the thermal-assisted strategy has been used to boost the transfer rate of carriers in photocatalytic reactions [17,18]. In contrast to external thermal-assisted photocatalysis, photothermal-assisted photocatalytic strategies need not consume additional energy besides free solar energy, which undoubtedly increased industrial value and attracted a lot of attention [19–21]. In our previous report, a black g-C3N4 (CN-B) photocatalyst was synthesized by the one-step calcination method of urea and phloxine B for improving the photothermal conversion capability, and then boosting the reaction temperature over the degradation of tetracycline TC in seawater under visible light irradiation [22]. Although the color change of g-C3N4 can effectively improve the effective use of light by the material, the rapid separation of light-generated electron holes is also an important indicator to improve the photocatalytic activity.

The introduction of photothermal-assisted co-catalyst can further improve the photothermal effect of the composite system and effectively extract the photo-induced charges generated by the semiconductor photocatalyst under light irradiation [23,24]. For instance, Yang et al. achieved a high photocatalytic H2 production rate by loading Mo2C photothermal nanoparticles with efficient photothermal conversion capability onto ZnIn2S4, and found that the photothermal effect promoted the separation of photo-generated carriers [25]. Sun et al. coated silver sulfide quantum dots (Ag2S QDs) with strong photothermal conversion efficiency on the surface of porous g-C3N4 nano vesicles (PCNNVs) to form Ag2S/PCNNVs nanoreactors by a simple calcination method for obtaining efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) evolution under simulated/real sunlight irradiation [26]. Thus, inspired by the above analysis, it is significant to further employ the photothermal co-catalyst to modify the black g-C3N4, so as to realize the synergistic effect of dual heat sources for increasing the temperature and accelerating the charge transport of the g-C3N4-based composite system though the volume/surface phase photothermal modifications.

Generally, carbon materials have excellent photothermal conversion ability and low synthesis cost, which is possible to replace metal-based photothermal materials to promote the improvement of photocatalytic activity [27,28]. As a new type of carbon-based functional nanomaterials, CDs have the advantages of low price, non-toxic and simple synthesis, which makes them possess the potential of industrial application [29–31]. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs) are often used as co-catalysts to modify semiconductor photocatalysts due to its unique optical properties and excellent electron transfer ability [32–34]. For example, Li et al. loaded CDs on the porous g-C3N4 nanovesicles to form CDs/PCNNVs and demonstrated that the loading of CDs not only modulates the energy band structure of CN, but also accelerates the migration of electrons [35]. Of greater concern, in our previous study, CDs were successfully loaded on the surface of the tubular g-C3N4 and studied the mechanism by which the photothermal effect of the CDs promoted the photocatalytic activity [36]. Consequently, it can be speculated that the combination of CN-B and CDs to form composite system could not only further improve the photothermal conversion efficiency, but also promote electron transfer, thus boosting the photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production.

In this work, CDs were loaded on the surface of porous CN-B nanosheets to form the CN-B-CDs composites by a simple calcination method for achieving high-efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production activity. Compared with pure CN, CN-B has a stronger photothermal effect and hydrophilicity, which promoted the transfer and separation of the carriers and the adsorption of H+, respectively. It is worth noting that the loading of the CDs not only further enhanced the photothermal effect of the material, but also optimized the transfer path of the carrier, which increased the rate of photocatalytic H2 production to 3.5 mmol g-1h−1. Combined with calculation and experiments, the photothermal conversion efficiency of CN-B-CDs and the correlation of their photocatalytic H2 production activity with temperature were explored. Finally, the mechanism of photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production over the CN-B-CDs composite system was discussed detail.

Urea (CO(NH2)2), phloxine B (C20H2Br4Cl4Na2O5), absolute ethyl alcohol, and triethanolamine ((HOCH2CH2)3N, TEOA) were purchased from Macklin Reagents Co., Ltd. and analytical grade without further purification. Graphite rods were purchased from Alibaba store.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a simple electrochemical route according to previous reports [37]. Briefly, an electrolytic cell was constructed with ultra-pure water as electrolyte and two graphite rods as electrodes, and a 30 V voltage was applied to it. After one week of continuous electrolysis, the obtained black solution was filtered several times to remove large-sized particles. Finally, the CDs powders was obtained by freeze-drying the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of black carbon nitride (CN-B) and pure carbon nitride (CN)
ce:section.ce:para: The black carbon nitride (CN-B) was synthesized by simple calcination and refined in previous reports [22]. Firstly, 10 mg of phloxine B powder was mixed with 10 g of urea in 30 mL of aqueous solution. After 30 min of stirring, the solution was transferred to a muffle furnace and heated to 520 ℃ at 5 ℃/min for 2 h. Finally, the CN-B powder was collected after natural cooling. The synthesis step of pure carbon nitride (CN) is similar to that of CN-B, but without adding phloxine B powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs modified CN-B (CN-B-CDs) composites
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of CDs modified CN-B (CN-B-CDs) composite was presented in the Fig. 1 . Firstly, a certain number of CDs powder (0.5, 1 and 2 mg) were sonicated in 30 mL of deionized water for 10 min to form the CDs solution. Then, 100 mg of CN-B was added to the CDs solution and stirred for 30 min. After uniform mixing, the resulting solution was transferred to a muffle furnace and heated at 10 ℃/min to 200 ℃ and maintained for 30 min. Furthermore, after natural cooling, CN-B-CDs composites were successfully prepared, and the resulting samples were named as CN-B-CDs-0.5, CN-B-CDs-1 and CN-B-CDs-2, respectively.

In addition, corresponding basic characterizations, detailed processes of photocatalytic H2 evolution tests, photothermal performance measurements, photo-electrochemical measurements and related calculation methods are provided in the Supporting Information .

The micromorphology of the synthesized photocatalysts was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to explore the effect of phloxine B and CDs on the morphology of CN. As given in Fig. 2 a-c, all the photocatalysts showed a curved sheet-like morphology, indicating that the morphology of g-C3N4 was not disrupted by the addition of CDs and phloxine B. To further explore the micromorphology of as-prepared photocatalysts, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) technique was employed. Observed from Fig. 2d-f, it is clear that all photocatalysts are distributed with irregular pores, caused by collision of gases during the thermal polymerization and believed to be beneficial to shortening the transfer path of photo-generated carriers [38]. In addition, it is clear that CN-B and CN-B-CDs have a smaller pore size compared to pure phase CN, which is believed to be due to the fact that the introduction of phloxine B during the thermal polymerization reaction affected the impact effect of the airflow. To verify the successful loading of the CDs, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was further tested. By further observing at Fig. 2g and h, numerous CDs are distributed on the surface of the CN nanosheets and the lattice fringes of CDs with a spacing of 0.21 nm can be found after amplification, which proved that the successful formation of CN-B-CDs composite photocatalyst [39]. Combining Fig.S1 and Table S1, it is obvious that all samples are composed of three elements of C, N and O, and the C element content in CN-B and CN-B-CDs composites are higher than that of pure CN, which may be one of the reasons for their darker color. Furthermore, the diverse C element content between composites is thought to be caused by differences in the amount of loaded CDs. And corresponding mapping images (Fig. 2i), revealing that three elements of C, N, and O are were evenly distributed on the sample surface.

To verify the successful synthesis of samples, the microstructure and chemical composition were explored by X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique. As exhibited in Fig. 3 a, the XRD patterns of all samples displayed two distinct characteristic diffraction peaks, corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystal surfaces of g-C3N4, caused by planar and interlayer stacking of the tris-triazine units, respectively [40–42]. For CN-B and CN-B-CDs composites, the corresponding XRD patterns showed similar profile to the pristine CN, which proved that the structure of CN can be well maintained. In Fig. S2b, there are still no significantly different exhibited in the XRD pattern of CN-B-CDs composites with different CDs content, which is believed to be caused by the few number or good dispersion of CDs. The structural features of the synthesized samples were further confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 3b, CN, CN-B and CN-B-CDs-1 all exhibited typical CN absorption peaks, again demonstrated that the CN structure can be well preserved. The strong characteristic sharp band at 810 cm−1 represents the typical tri-s-triazine rings, and absorption bands at 1840–920 cm−1 and 3650–2840 cm−1 ascribed to stretching vibrations of CN and NH, respectively [43–45]. The FT-IR vibration characteristics of CN-B-CDs composites with different CDs content are still analogous, which again proved that the loading of CDs has very little effect on the structure of CN (Fig. S2b) [46]. To explore the effect of the modification of the CDs on the specific surface area of the composites, the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the samples were tested. As given in Fig. 3c, it is noticed that the typical type IV with an H3 hysteresis loop again confirmed the presence of pores in the samples. CN-B-CDs-1 exhibited the highest specific surface area, which is believed to be caused by the effective dispersion of CDs on the surface of CN and this higher surface area favored the adsorption of the photocatalyst to the reactants. There was no significant alteration in the specific surface area of CN-B compared to CN, which again indicates that the addition of phloxine B during the process of synthesis had little effect on the structure of CN. The pore size distributions in Fig.S3 revealed that compared to CN, CN-B and CN-B-CDs-1 exhibited smaller pore sizes, which are consistent with the observations of TEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to investigate the compositions and chemical states in synthesized photocatalysts. All photocatalysts are composed of three elements (C, N and O), and the amount of C elements increased after the addition of phloxine B and CDs for CN-B and CN-B-CDs (Fig.S4). Moreover, the relatively abundant content of oxygen elements in the CN-B-CDs-1 composite is believed to result from the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs [47,48]. To further demonstrate the state of the elements, the high-resolution XPS spectra C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s for pristine CN, CN-B and CN-B-CDs-1 samples were collected. In Fig. 3d, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s for CN-B-CDs-1 were detected with three peaks corresponding to CC, CNH and C-NC, and located at 284.8, 285.9 and 288.4 eV, respectively [49,50]. The CNH bond in CN-B-CDs-1 was detected with lower binding energy compared to CN and CN-B, which can be attributed to the interaction between CDs and CN [35]. For the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum, CN-B-CDs-1 was detected with four peaks with binding energies of 398.8, 399.4, 401.2 and 404.5 eV, and corresponding to the C-NC, N-(C)3 group, NH bond and π-excited state, respectively (Fig. 3e) [51]. Compared with pure CN, both CN-B and CN-B-CDs-1 showed lower binding energies of N-(C)3 and NH bond, which represented the altered chemical environment and electronic structure of CN, and may result from the introduction of carbon elements from phloxine B [22,52–54]. In Fig. 3f, the peaks detected by CN-B-CDs-1 at 532.5 and 531.8 eV can be attributed to the mixing peaks of oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs and adsorbed oxygen, and the oxygen elements of both CN-B and CN are derived from adsorbed oxygen [15,35,55]. Considering that the surface oxygen-containing functional groups may affect the surface properties of the CN, the water contact angle of the photocatalysts was tested. As given in Fig. 3g-i, different with CN, both CN-B and CN-B-CDs showed a water contact angle of less than 90°, which indicated that the surface properties of CN are improved under the action of phloxine B. Notably, CN-B-CDs-1 showed the strongest hydrophilicity, which is believed to be due to the oxygen-containing functional group on the CDs surface promoting the interaction between the photocatalyst and water, and this high hydrophilic nature facilitates the forward progress of the photocatalytic H2 production [56–58].

Optical absorption characteristics of catalysts are an important factor affecting photocatalytic activity. As exhibited in Fig.S5 both CN-B and CN-B-CDs photocatalysts are black, which means a higher visible light absorption ability compared to ordinary CN. To prove the above view, the UV–vis absorption spectra of all the samples were tested. As displayed in the Fig. 4 a, CN-B showed superior light absorption ability and longer light absorption edge, which could be conducive to the improvement of photothermal conversion ability and the generation of charge carriers [59–62]. Moreover, the visible light absorption ability of CN-B was further enhanced by the addition of CDs and exhibited a positive correlation with the content of CDs (Fig.S6a), which indicated that the load of CDs is expected to further improve the photothermal conversion capacity of CN-B. According to the UV–vis absorption spectra, the band gap of samples was calculated by using the Kubelka-Munk equation, as displayed in Fig. 4b, the band gap values of CN-B and CN-B-CDs composite were significantly narrower compared to pure CN, indicating that synergistic reaction of the doping of carbon caused by addition of phloxine B and the loading of carbon caused by CDs can affect the electronic binding of CN [63]. Moreover, the band gaps of CN-B-CDs composites becomes significantly narrowed with the increasing CDs content (Fig.S6b). To further probe the band structure of as-prepared CN, CN-B and CN-B-CDs, the valence band (VB) potentials were confirmed by VB-XPS. In Fig.S7, based on the contact potential difference between samples and analyzer, the VB potentials of CN, CN-B, and CN-B-CDs-1 were localized at 2.46, 2.26 and 2.52 eV, and finally calculated as 2.32, 2.12, and 2.38 V vs. NHE by formula E VB-NHE = Φ + E VB-XPS – 4.44, where the Ф is the work function of the instrument (4.3 eV) [64]. Combined with the VB and band gap of the photocatalyst, the potentials of the conduction band (CB) of CN, CN-B and CN-B-CDs-1 are calculated to be −0.65, −0.8 and −0.43 V respectively, and the band structures are provided in Fig. 4c. Obviously, although the addition of phloxine B and the modification of CDs narrowed the band gap of CN to varying degrees, all the photocatalysts satisfy the thermodynamic conditions for photocatalytic H2 production. For the photocatalytic reactions, the enhanced temperature caused by photothermal effect is often conducive to the improvement of reaction dynamics, while the photothermal materials usually have a strong wide spectrum absorption ability [65,66]. In this regard, CN-B sample presents a black sample color, meanwhile the introduction of CDs can further promote the color deepening of the composite sample, which can be effectively used as a photothermal promoter to raise the temperature of the composite system and accelerate the effective transport of light-generated charges. As a result, the temperature changes of different as-prepared samples under full-spectrum irradiation were recorded by the infrared thermal imager to compare the photothermal conversion capacity, and subsequently the photothermal conversion efficiency was explored. As displayed in Fig. 4d, after 5 min of illumination, the surface temperature of CN-B and CN-B-CDs-1 powders reached 79.2 and 89.0 ℃ respectively, much higher than that of CN, indicating that synergistic reaction of the doping of carbon caused by addition of phloxine B and the loading of carbon caused by CDs can also boost the photothermal conversion capacity of composite system. Consistent with the changing trend of optical absorption capacity, the photothermal conversion capacity of CN-B-CDs composite photocatalysts gradually increased with the increase of CDs content (Fig.S8). Since the photocatalytic reaction was performed in suspension, a suspension of CN-B-CDs-1 at 0.5 mg/mL concentration was prepared and used to study the internal photothermal conversion efficiency of CN-B-CDs-1 composites. The temperature change curves of the suspension under light and dark conditions are exhibited in Fig. 4e, and the time constant (τs) and the internal photothermal conversion efficiency (ηint) are calculated. Under the illumination of 300 W xenon lamp, the photothermal conversion efficiency of optimum CN-B-CDs-1 reached 86.9 %, which further proved the strong photothermal conversion ability.

Photocatalytic H2 production experiments were performed on all the prepared catalysts in order to evaluate their photocatalytic activity. In the control experiment, the H2 production rate without photocatalyst under light irradiation or photocatalyst without light irradiation exhibited 0 mmol g-1h−1 (Fig.S9), indicating that all H2 is generated by photocatalytic reaction. As can be seen in Fig. 5 a, in the 2 h of photocatalytic H2 production experiment, all the photocatalysts have a certain photocatalytic H2 production activity, and the amount of H2 increased steadily with the reaction time. Based on the results of the photocatalytic H2 production experiments, the photocatalytic H2 production rates of all of the samples were calculated and presented in Fig. 5b. It is clearly that CN-B exhibited a higher H2 production rate than that of ordinary CN, which is thought to result from the stronger photothermal effect of CN-B. It is worth noting that the photocatalytic H2 production rate of prepared CN-B-CDs composites were somewhat higher than that of CN-B and CN photocatalysts, perhaps due to the CDs acting as a cocatalyst but also as a photothermal material for enhancing the photo-generated carriers’ transfer rate. Comparing the CN-B-CDs composites with different CDs content, the CN-B-CDs-1 photocatalyst displaced highest photocatalytic H2 production rate, reaching about 3.5 mmol g-1h−1. In Fig. 5c, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQY) of the CN-B-CDs-1 photocatalyst at 420 nm is about 9.21 %, which is a high level for the CN-based photocatalysts. Moreover, it is clear that the AQY of CN-B-CDs-1 at 550 and 650 nm does not match the UV–visible absorption spectrum, which is thought to be resulted from the absorbed light being mainly converted to thermal for accelerating the generation of carriers. To explore the temperature rise caused by the photothermal effects during photocatalysis, the temperature changes of different photocatalytic systems under illumination were recorded. As can be seen in Fig. 5d, after 2 h of illumination, the temperature of the pure CN photocatalytic system reached 37.9 ℃, which is only 4.3 ℃ higher than that of the pure water (Fig.S10). Unlike the CN suspension, the temperature of the CN-B suspension was up to 40.1℃ after 2 h of illumination (Fig. 5e), mainly caused by the strong photothermal conversion ability of the CN-B. Compared with all the photocatalytic systems tested, the CN-B-CDs-1 suspension reached the highest final temperature (Fig. 5f), which once again proved that the loading of CDs further enhanced the photothermal conversion ability of the photocatalyst [67]. Although a positive correlation between photocatalytic activity and photothermal conversion ability was exhibited by compare different photocatalyst systems, control variables are still needed to more accurately explore the relationship between temperature and reaction kinetics. Consequently, the photocatalytic H2 production activity of the photocatalysts under different temperature conditions was tested, and the test results were presented in Fig. 5g and h. At the same temperature, CN-B exhibited a H2 production rate higher than that of pure CN, indicating that the hydrophilicity and the pore size of the photocatalyst are also important factors to change the photocatalytic activity. Alternatively, CN-B-CDs-1 exhibited the highest H2 production rate under any temperature condition, and it is believed to be due to the transfer of photo-generated charges facilitated by the modification of CDs. It is worth noting that the H2 production rate of all photocatalysts gradually increased as the temperature increases, proving that the improvement of temperature can effectively promote the reaction kinetics. Based on the results of H2 production at different temperatures, the activation energy of the photocatalytic reaction over as-prepared samples was calculated and presented in Fig. 5i. The reaction activation energies of CN, CN-B, and CN-B-CDs-1 reached 47.13, 36.77 and 31.24 kJ/mol, respectively, indicating that the photothermal effects induced by CN-B and CDs significantly promoted the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction [68,69]. To explore the potential value of CN-B-CDs photocatalysts for industrial application, photocatalysis tests were performed under different conditions and the test results are presented in Fig. 5j-l. In the H2 production tests with different water bodies, different photocatalytic activities were displayed (Fig. 5j), which are believed to be caused by different impurities. It is worth noting that the photocatalytic activity of CN-B-CDs-1 photocatalysts is well maintained in tap water and seawater, implying a wide range of water resources. In Fig. 5k, the photocatalytic activity of CN-B-CDs-1 reached the highest value at pH of 5.5 and gradually weakened as the pH increases or decreases, because the high H+ concentration leaded to the oxidation of the sacrificial agent and the low H+ concentration reduced the adsorption of the photocatalyst to the H+. By testing the photocatalytic H2 production rate of CN-B-CDs-1 photocatalysts under different light conditions, it was found that CN-B-CDs-1 displayed the highest photocatalytic activity under ultraviolet (UV) light illumination and had certain visible (Vis) light response ability (Fig. 5l). In addition, there is no H2 production under the irradiation of near infrared (NIR) light, which can be attributed because the absorbed NIR light is mainly used for photothermal conversion rather than carriers’ production [59]. In order to further explore the application potential of CN-B-CDs-1 photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production system, when compared with other reports, the results revealed that the photocatalytic activity of CN-B-CDs-1 is higher than that of most CN-based photocatalysts (Fig. 5m and Table S2). In addition, the 4-cycle H2 production test proved that the CN-B-CDs-1 photocatalytic H2 production system possesses strong stability (Fig. 5n), which is also an important factor affecting the value of industrial application. In addition, Fig.S11 compare the XRD patterns of CN-B-CDs-1 photocatalyst before and after photocatalysis, which further confirms that there is no obvious structural change of the used CN-B-CDs, indicating that the prepared CN-B-CDs materials possess good stability.

In order to understand the mechanism of photothermal effects for promoting photocatalytic reactions, the separation and transfer process of photo-generated carriers under different temperature and light conditions were explored. As seen from Fig. 6 a, compared with that of pure CN, photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CN-B had a weaker characteristic peak at the same temperature, meaning that the recombination of photo-generated carriers in CN-B was inhibited by doping of carbon caused by addition of phloxine B. In addition, CN-B-CDs-1 exhibited the weakest characteristic peaks under the same temperature conditions, which is thought to result from the loading of CDs effectively promoted the separation of photo-generated carriers [39,70]. All samples presented higher charge separation efficiency at 50 ℃ than at room temperature (RT), indicating that the strong photothermal effect favors the formation of electron and hole pairs. Meanwhile, all samples at 50 ℃ exhibited a small EIS arc radius (Fig. 6b), meaning that the increased temperature induced by the photothermal effect is conducive to accelerating the charge transfer. At the same temperature, CN-B exhibited a smaller impedance than pure CN, mainly due to the improved charge transfer path provided by the doped carbon. Notably, the EIS arc radius of CN-B-CDs-1 is significantly smaller than that of the other samples, providing strong evidence that the loading of CDs promoted charge transfer [71,72]. To more intuitively investigate the photothermal effect on the behavior of photo-generated carriers, photocurrent response curves were tested and presented in Fig. 6c and d. Obviously, CN-B-CDs-1 exhibited a stronger photocurrent density at 50 ℃ compared to at RT (Fig. 6c), further indicating that the higher temperature is more favorable for the transfer of photo-generated carriers. In Fig. 6d, the current density of CN-B-CDs-1 under UV and Vis-NIR illumination reached 0.5 and 0.07 μA cm−2, respectively, but did not exhibit optical response ability under NIR illumination, verifying that photo-generated carriers are mainly excited by high-frequency photons. More notably, the current density of CN-B-CDs is as high as 6.5 μA cm−2 in the full spectrum light illumination, even higher than that of the sum under UV and Vis-NIR light illumination, which can be attributed to the synergistic effect of low-frequency photons induced photothermal effect with high-frequency photons induced charge separation [25,59,73]. Alternatively, the Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots of the samples under dark or light conditions were tested and used to study their carriers’ densities. As can be seen in Fig.S12, the slope of the M−S curve of the CN-B is significantly smaller under the light compared to the dark conditions, while little change occurs on the pure CN, which is thought to be due to the charge carriers’ densities enhanced by the photothermal effect of the CN-B. Compared to CN and CN-B, CN-B-CDs exhibited a smaller slope in the dark condition, and the slope is further reduced in the light condition (Fig. 6e), which represents that the load of CDs significantly increased the carrier density in the catalyst and maintained a strong photothermal effect. Based on the test results of the M−S plots, the calculated carrier density of CN-B-CDs-1 reached 15.8 × 1023 cm−3 under the light condition, much higher than its value in the dark condition (Fig. 6f), which provided evidence that the photothermal effect can greatly promote the separation and transfer of the carriers.

Combining the basic characterization and experimental analysis, a possible mechanism of CN-B-CDs composite photocatalyst for photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production was proposed in Fig. 7 . Due to the loading of the CDs, the free e- generated by the irradiation of the high-frequency photons is rapidly transferred from the CB of CN-B to the CDs quickly to inhibit charge recombination, while the h+ at the VB is rapidly absorbed by TEOA. The rich oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs enhanced the hydrophilic of the photocatalyst, leading to the abundant H+ being adsorbed and quickly reacted with e- to produce H2. In addition, benefit by the strong photothermal conversion capacity, a part of the low-frequency photons is absorbed by CN-B-CDs and generated heat to raise the temperature of the reaction system, which not only accelerated the charge transfer but also broadened the utilization range of the spectrum. It is worth noting that although CN-B has a strong photothermal conversion capability, the load of CDs still allows it to further increase, which has been proved to further increase the concentration of carriers. Overall, in this mechanism, the efficient photothermal conversion combined with the traction ability of CDs to carriers facilitated the migration and separation of photo-generated carriers during photocatalysis.

In conclusion, a CN-B-CDs composite photocatalyst with highly efficient photothermal conversion capability was prepared by a simple calcination method for achieving highly efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalytic H2 production research. The test results revealed that CN-B-CDs photocatalyst have larger specific surface area, favorable charge transfer path and higher hydrophilicity compared with pure CN, which facilitates the promotion of photocatalytic activity. More importantly, the low-frequency photons can be converted into heat by the powerful photothermal effects to accelerate the separation and transfer of charges, which expands the utilization range of the solar spectrum. In addition, the loading of CDs not only greatly improved the carrier transfer speed, but also further improved the photothermal conversion efficiency, leading to the rapid photocatalytic reaction dynamics. A series of performance tests proved that the photocatalytic activity of CN-B-CDs is higher than most reported CN-based photocatalysts and suitable for many different water bodies, verifying its potential industrial applications. This study not only designed a photocatalyst with efficient photothermal conversion capability, but also provided new ideas for achieving efficient photothermal-assisted photocatalysis using carbon-based materials.
